source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Ensure Cloud Object Storage bucket access is restricted by using IAM and S3 access control

input_row.source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Ensure Cloud Object Storage bucket access is restricted by using IAM and S3 access control
input_row.description: Access controls on the Cloud Object Storage buckets are governed via IBM Identity and Access Management (IAM). However, some permissions can also be granted (or restricted) via S3 access controls.
input_row.rationale: Assign IAM access roles for users and Service IDs against buckets, by using either the UI or the CLI to create policies. The S3 Access Control is performed using ACLs granted against buckets and objects. Impact: Organizations can use Cloud Object Storage to store sensitive data that may need to be restricted. Therefore, creation of a new Cloud Object Storage bucket requires careful review and planning around access controls provided by IBM Cloud environment to ensure data access can be restricted to only those having valid business need.
input_row.audit: Reviewing assigned access using IAM Complete the following steps to review your assigned access in an account that you have been added to: 1. In the console, click Manage > Access (IAM) , and select Users or Manage > Access (IAM) , and select Service IDs , depending on which identity you want to review. 2. Select your name or the service ID. 3. Review the assigned access in the Access policies section. Reviewing S3 ACLs to buckets View the S3 ACLs via API or CLI. A typical API call is shown: curl -X "GET" "https://{endpoint}/{bucket-name}?acl" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer {token}" \
input_row.remediation: Granting Access to a COS Bucket using IAM Using the UI To create a new bucket-level policy: 1. Navigate to the Access IAM console from the Manage menu. 2. Select Users from the left navigation menu. 3. Select a user. 4. Select the Access Policies tab to view the user's existing policies, assign a new policy, or edit an existing policy. 5. Click Assign access to create a new policy. 6. Choose Assign access to resources . 7. First, select Cloud Object Storage from the services menu. 8. Then, select the appropriate service instance. Enter bucket in the Resource type field and the bucket name in the Resource ID field. 9. Select the wanted service access role. Selecting the lozenge with the number of actions show the actions available to the role. 10. Click Assign Using the CLI From a terminal run the following command: ibmcloud iam user-policy-create <user-name> \ --roles <role> \ --service-name cloud-object-storage \ --service-instance <resource-instance-id> \ --resource-type bucket \ --resource <bucket-name> To list existing policies: ibmcloud iam user-policies <user-name> To edit an existing policy: ibmcloud iam user-policy-update <user-name> <policy-id> \ --roles <role> \ --service-name cloud-object-storage \ --service-instance <resource-instance-id> \ --resource-type bucket \ --resource <bucket-name> The same set of accesses can be assigned to a service id as well. Please follow the procedure documented in https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cloud-object-storage?topic=cloud- object-storage-iam-bucket-permissions#iam-service-id Granting S3 ACLs to buckets Grant the S3 ACLs via API or CLI. A Typical API call is shown: curl -X "PUT" "https://{endpoint}/{bucket-name}?acl" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer {token}" \ -H "x-amz-acl: <scope>" Default Value: By default, users or service ids which have Cloud Object Storage service role access to the Cloud Object Storage service instances will have access to the Cloud Object Storage buckets under them as well. References: 1. • https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cloud-object-storage?topic=cloud-object-storage- iam-bucket-permissions 2. • https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/account?topic=account-assign-access-resources 3. • https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cloud-object-storage?topic=cloud-object-storage- iam-public-access#public-access-object
input_row.impact: Organizations can use Cloud Object Storage to store sensitive data that may need to be restricted. Therefore, creation of a new Cloud Object Storage bucket requires careful review and planning around access controls provided by IBM Cloud environment to ensure data access can be restricted to only those having valid business need.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The audit approach for control 2.1.4, which ensures that Cloud Object Storage bucket access is restricted via IAM and S3 access control, can be automated using the IBM Cloud APIs and CLI. Here's why:

✓ The IBM Cloud APIs provide the ability to query IAM access policies and S3 ACLs related to Cloud Object Storage.
✓ This control specifically deals with access control management for Cloud Object Storage, a standard IBM Cloud service property, which can be managed through APIs.
✓ The IBM Cloud CLI includes commands to retrieve and manage IAM policies, which are relevant to this control.
✓ The compliance check involves IAM and S3 ACLs, which are part of IBM Cloud's standard services where configurations can be queried and managed through APIs and CLI.
✓ There is no requirement for SSH/RDP access to instances as the processes involve querying access configurations rather than inspecting application code or OS configs.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_cos_bucket_access_control_restrictions

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

1. **IBM Cloud API/CLI Usage:**
   - Utilize the IBM Cloud CLI to check current IAM policies assigned to users and service IDs for a specific Cloud Object Storage bucket.
     - `ibmcloud iam user-policies <user-name>` to list existing policies related to Cloud Object Storage buckets.
   - Use `curl` or relevant IBM Cloud CLI for querying S3 ACLs directly from the API to ensure no permissive access configurations (like public access) are set.
     - Example API call to check S3 ACLs:
       ```
       curl -X "GET" "https://{endpoint}/{bucket-name}?acl" \
       -H "Authorization: Bearer {token}"
       ```

2. **Resources to Check:**
   - Enumerate all Cloud Object Storage buckets across the account.
   - For each bucket, retrieve and verify the IAM policies assigned.
   - Check each bucket's S3 ACL configurations.

3. **Configuration Validation:**
   - Ensure that IAM policies only grant necessary access roles to users and service IDs.
   - Confirm that S3 ACLs do not allow unauthorized users to access buckets or objects.

4. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** IAM policies are restrictive to only required users/service IDs with specified roles. S3 ACLs do not provide permissions beyond those necessary, especially no public access.
   - **Fail:** If IAM policies provide excessive permissions or if S3 ACLs allow overly permissive access.

By automating this control check with the IBM Cloud static analysis tools and commands, organizations can continuously monitor and ensure compliance with the CIS benchmark for secure Cloud Object Storage configurations.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The IBM Cloud APIs and CLI provide sufficient capabilities to automate the checking of IAM policies and S3 ACLs for Cloud Object Storage buckets. The use of these tools aligns with the requirement to ensure access controls are properly configured, without needing manual inspection.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly accurate. The use of `ibmcloud iam user-policies <user-name>` is valid for retrieving IAM policies, but it might be more effective to use `ibmcloud iam access-policies` to directly target policies associated with specific resources like Cloud Object Storage.
- The `curl` command for querying S3 ACLs is technically correct, but it assumes the user has the necessary permissions and a valid token, which should be explicitly mentioned.
- The program name `ibm_cos_bucket_access_control_restrictions` is descriptive, but it could be more specific by including terms like 'iam_s3' to indicate both IAM and S3 ACL checks.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** Consider renaming to `ibm_cos_iam_s3_access_control_check` for clarity on the specific checks being performed.
- **Additional Checks:** It would be beneficial to include checks for bucket policies, which can also affect access controls. This could be done using the IBM Cloud CLI or API to ensure comprehensive coverage.
- **Token Management:** Include steps or references for obtaining and managing the Bearer token required for API calls, ensuring users understand how to authenticate properly.
- **Error Handling:** Consider adding error handling mechanisms in the automation script to manage cases where API calls fail or return unexpected results.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded and aligns with IBM Cloud's capabilities for automating compliance checks. The use of IBM Cloud APIs and CLI is appropriate for this task, and the proposed automation approach is technically sound with minor improvements suggested.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Update the program name to `ibm_cos_iam_s3_access_control_check` for better clarity. Include additional checks for bucket policies and provide guidance on token management and error handling in the automation script. These modifications will enhance the robustness and clarity of the audit process.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identified that IBM Cloud provides robust APIs and CLI commands to programmatically retrieve and evaluate IAM access policies and S3 ACLs for Cloud Object Storage buckets. All required properties for this control (IAM policies, S3 ACLs, and optionally bucket policies) are exposed via IBM Cloud CLI and API, making full automation feasible without manual console inspection.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
ibm_cos_bucket_iam_s3_access_control_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- IBM Cloud Resource/Service: Cloud Object Storage (COS), IAM Policies, S3 ACLs
- API/CLI Method:
  - List IAM access policies for COS buckets:  
    `ibmcloud iam access-policies --resource-type cloud-object-storage`
  - List S3 ACLs for each bucket:  
    `ibmcloud cos bucket-acl-get --bucket <bucket-name> --region <region>`
  - (Optional) List bucket policies:  
    `ibmcloud cos bucket-policy-get --bucket <bucket-name> --region <region>`
- Properties to Check:
  - IAM: Ensure only authorized users/service IDs have access to COS buckets, with least privilege.
  - S3 ACLs: Ensure no public or overly permissive grants (e.g., "AllUsers" or "AuthenticatedUsers" with read/write).
  - Bucket Policies: Ensure no policies grant public or excessive access.
- Pass Criteria:
  - All COS buckets have access restricted to intended users/service IDs via IAM.
  - No S3 ACLs or bucket policies grant public or unauthorized access.
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any COS bucket is accessible by unauthorized users, public, or has overly permissive S3 ACLs or bucket policies.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation, with the second suggesting a more descriptive program name and inclusion of bucket policies. I adopted these improvements for completeness and clarity.

**SOURCE:** CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
